title: Variance Reduction: 2× Signal Exposure Cuts CV from 29% to 20%

content.finding: Of three variance reduction approaches tested, only longer signal exposure (n_perm=8, n_trials=6, doubling both) reduces CV meaningfully: 29.2% → 19.6% (1.79× improvement). Within-seed paired design INCREASES variance (CV=91.9%), and adding K values INCREASES variance (CV=48.2%).
content.evidence: Tested on canonical baseline across 10 seeds. (1) Paired design: difference of two noisy measurements has double variance, CV=91.9%. (2) 2× exposure: n_perm=8, n_trials=6, CV=19.6%. (3) Extended K=[3,4,6,8,10,12]: K=3,12 add noise without averaging benefit, CV=48.2%.
implications: Future experiments should use n_perm=8, n_trials=6 (4× runtime cost)
With CV=20%, minimum detectable effect drops from ~8% to ~5%
This does NOT rescue Candidate #2 or delta_rz (effect sizes ~0-2%)
Still need ~50 seeds for effects <5%, but 10-seed experiments can now detect 5%+ effects
content.key_insight: Variance reduction through longer exposure is the only viable path. Paired designs fail because both conditions share the same noise sources. Extended K values fail because extreme K adds more noise than averaging removes.

constraints_implied: Future experiments must use n_perm=8, n_trials=6 (2× exposure) to achieve CV≈20%
Within-seed paired design increases variance — do not use
Adding extreme K values (K=3,12) increases variance — stick to K=[4,6,8,10]
